I hammer the jagged shards of fallen dreadnoughts into crude blades, for the empire has forgotten how to forge steel and now only knows how to scrap it.
While the high lords squabble over the dying embers of their throne worlds, I spend my days tempering armor that can withstand the desperate raids of starving colonists.
I can tell by the purity of the ore that this shipment came from a sector already lost to the void, yet I treat the metal with a reverence the governors no longer show their people.
My forge is the only heat left in this frozen outpost, turning the discarded husks of ancient androids into the tools we need to survive the coming dark.
I graft circuitry and iron into the limbs of broken soldiers, stitching together a fragile peace with a soldering iron while the galactic borders dissolve into chaos.
I hammer the jagged edges of these conflicting corporate cultures until they fuse into a single, unbreakable entity.
I cast the initial term sheet into the fire, purging the weaknesses of the original offer to temper a stronger deal.
I spend my nights at the anvil of the boardroom, folding the assets of both companies over and over to remove every impurity.
I strike while the iron is hot, forcing the reluctant board members to yield under the weight of my heavy gavel.
I quench the white-hot tension of the hostile takeover in a sudden flood of cold, hard capital.
I hammer the red-hot ore into jagged spearheads, knowing each strike prepares my kin for the coming slaughter.
My forge burns with a heat that rivals the anger of the rival clan crossing our riverbeds.
I refuse to mend the broken shields of those who would betray our tribal elders for a handful of salt.
The rhythmic clang of my anvil serves as a war drum, signaling to the valley that our steel is ready.
I watch the warriors argue over boundaries, knowing that the strength of their resolve depends entirely on the edge of my blade.
I hammer the jagged edges of the city's subconscious, forging raw neural surges into stable architecture for the dreaming districts.
When the central processor suffers a fracture, I dive into the molten data-streams to weld the fragmented logic gates back together.
I shape the city's kinetic defense grids, tempering the translucent energy shields until they can withstand the pressure of a collapsing star.
My anvil is the heartbeat of the subterranean core, where I beat raw gravity into precise instruments for the floating spires.
I refine the sentient alloys of the transit veins, sculpting the living metal so it may flow seamlessly around the desires of the populace.
I have spent an eternity hammering the spectral shackles that bind the damned to their witness stands.
My anvil echoes through this silent chamber, forging the unbreakable seals that prevent the deceased from lying under oath.
I stand before the High Arbiter, offering a blade forged from solidified grief to sever the silver cord of the accused.
The heat of my forge is the only thing capable of melting the frozen hearts presented as evidence in this ghostly tribunal.
I carefully shape the iron crowns of judgment, ensuring every spike is perfectly aligned to pierce the ego of the fallen.
I hammer these salvaged titanium struts into reinforced plating to keep the crushing pressure of the midnight zone from folding this hull like paper.
The rhythmic clang of my forge echoes through the flooded corridors, drowning out the unsettling groans of the station's dying support beams.
I treat the leaking geothermal vents as my furnace, coaxing the white-hot heat of the earth to soften the scrap metal I scavenge from the ruins.
My anvil is a repurposed airlock hatch, the only surface heavy enough to withstand the force I need to reshape these rusted oxygen scrubbers.
I spend my hours welding makeshift patches onto the breached bulkheads, fighting a losing war against the salt that eats through my iron and my patience.
I stare at the minimalist white walls and wonder why these artists treat empty space as a sculpture when I treat raw iron as a void to be filled.
The conceptual descriptions beside these pieces are far too wordy, for the true language of creation is found in the rhythmic, deafening scream of a hammer meeting an anvil.
I find it curious that these visitors admire the "industrial aesthetic" of rusted beams while ignoring the calloused hands and scorched skin required to bend such stubborn matter.
While the curator speaks of ephemeral installations, I think only of the eternal permanence of a weld that refuses to break under a thousand pounds of pressure.
I feel a strange kinship with the distorted metal sculptures in this gallery, knowing that I alone understand the exact temperature of the fire that forced them into such agony.
I spend my days forging heavy iron bolts and reinforced bars to seal the healthy families inside their homes and keep the dying at bay.
My forge is the only place in the village where the heat is intense enough to burn away the stench of decay clinging to my leather apron.
I no longer shape horseshoes or plowshares, for the farmers have all perished and there is no one left to till the blackened earth.
I hammered out a crude, iron-reinforced mask for the plague doctor, hoping the metal would be a stronger barrier than mere leather and wax.
As the bells toll for the dead, I strike my anvil with a rhythmic fury, trying to drown out the screams of those succumbing to the swellings.
I strike the anvil with all my might, but the sound is a lagging echo that repeats three times before the spark even flies.
My hammer passed straight through the glowing iron as if the sword had momentarily lost its collision data.
I watched in horror as the embers of my forge froze in mid-air, suspended in a jagged, pixelated snapshot of a fire that forgot how to burn.
The steel I folded a thousand times suddenly shifted its texture, turning into a shimmering, low-resolution void that consumes the light of the workshop.
I reached for my tongs, only to find my arm stretching across the room in a distorted smear of geometry that defied every law of physics I know.
